title: Treasury Releases Report Showing U.S. Childcare System Overburdens Families and Causes Shortages Due to Inadequate Supply
summary: The U.S. Treasury released a report on September 15, 2021, analyzing the child care market, finding that families with children under 5 spend approximately 13% of income on child care, well above the 7% target proposed under the Build Back Better agenda. The report attributes market failures to liquidity constraints and positive externalities, arguing for government intervention through universal preschool and expanded tax credits.
key_points: Treasury report authored by acting Assistant Secretary Catherine Wolfram examines childcare market failures
Average family with child under 5 spends ~13% of income on childcare vs proposed 7% target
Report identifies liquidity constraints and positive externalities as market failures justifying government support
Build Back Better proposals include universal preschool for 3-4 year olds and expanded Child and Dependent Care Tax Credit
Secretary Yellen stated childcare is essential to economic growth comparable to infrastructure or energy
why_it_matters: This source discusses fiscal policy proposals (childcare subsidies, Build Back Better agenda) rather than market-moving economic data or financial market catalysts. It is not relevant to trading, interest rate policy, or financial market dynamics.
